items.title: HBV serological markers
items.contentHtml:
Serological markers Hepatitis B

  • HBsAg: marker of active infection (acute/chronic); persists >6 months defines chronicity.
  • Anti-HBs: neutralizing antibody; detectable after resolved infection or vaccination (>10 IU/mL = protection).
  • Anti-HBc IgG: past infection; Anti-HBc IgM: acute infection (serological window).
  • HBeAg: active replication and high infectivity; Anti-HBe: seroconversion (precore mutant).
  • HBV DNA: viral load; confirms replication and guides treatment (>2000 IU/mL in HBeAg-).

items.title: VHB window period
items.contentHtml:
Hepatitis B window period

  • Interval between disappearance of HBsAg and appearance of Anti-HBs.
  • Only detectable marker in this phase: Anti-HBc IgM (acute infection).
  • Typical duration: 2–6 weeks; It can be prolonged in subclinical infections.
  • Exam trap: patient with acute hepatitis, HBsAg negative, Anti-HBs negative → request Anti-HBc IgM.
  • If omitted, false diagnosis of non-HBV hepatitis.

items.title: HBV precore mutant
items.contentHtml:
Hepatitis B precore mutant

  • G1896A mutation in the HBV precore region, prevents HBeAg production.
  • Phenotype: HBeAg negative, Anti-HBe positive, but active replication (high HBV DNA).
  • Associated with genotype D (Mediterranean, Asia); increased risk of fulminant hepatitis and cirrhosis.
  • Diagnosis: suspect if HBeAg(-) with viral load >2000 IU/mL and elevated ALT.
  • Treatment: same antivirals as HBV HBeAg+ (tenofovir/entecavir).

items.title: Hepatitis B vaccination
items.contentHtml:
Hepatitis B Vaccination

  • 3 dose schedule (0-1-6 months); via IM in deltoids.
  • Response: Anti-HBs >10 IU/mL 1–2 months after last dose.
  • Non-responders: repeat complete cycle; If it persists without response, rule out occult infection (HBsAg, HBV DNA).
  • Dose: adults 20 µg (10 µg children); Engerix-B or Recombivax.
  • Target population: newborns, sexual contacts, healthcare personnel, HIV, dialysis.
  • Post-exposure: HBV immunoglobulin + vaccine (first 12–24 hours).

items.title: Chronic HBV treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment Chronic Hepatitis B

  • Indication: HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL + elevated ALT (or fibrosis ≥F2).
  • First line: Tenofovir disoproxil (300 mg/day) or Entecavir (0.5 mg/day); both with high genetic barrier.
  • Alternative: Tenofovir alafenamide (25 mg/day) in patients with renal/bone risk.
  • Duration: HBeAg+ until durable seroconversion (≥12 months consolidation); HBeAg- undefined.
  • Do not use interferon in decompensated cirrhosis; avoid lamivudine due to resistance.

items.title: HCV genotypes
items.contentHtml:
Hepatitis C Genotypes

  • 7 main genotypes (1–7); genotype 1 is the most prevalent globally (≈46%).
  • Genotype 1b: most common in Europe/America; genotype 3: associated with hepatic steatosis and higher risk of cirrhosis.
  • Genotype 2 and 3: better response to interferon (pre-AAD era); today all treatable with DAAs.
  • Genotype 4: common in Africa/Middle East; genotype 5 and 6: specific regions.
  • Determination: necessary to choose AAD regimen and duration (8–12 weeks).

items.foco: RNA and viral load Hepatitis C
items.title: HCV RNA and viral load
items.contentHtml:
RNA and viral load Hepatitis C

  • HCV-RNA quantitative: confirms active infection; detectable from 1–2 weeks post-exposure.
  • Unit: IU/mL; viral load <800,000 IU/mL = low; ≥800,000 IU/mL = high (relevant in genotypes 1 and 4 in the interferon era).
  • Sustained viral response (SVR): HCV-RNA undetectable 12 weeks after completing treatment (virological cure).
  • Monitoring: baseline, week 4, end of treatment and SVR12.
  • Viral load does not predict fibrosis but does predict response to treatment. (AAD: efficiency >95% independent of load).

items.title: Genotype 1 and HCV resistance
items.contentHtml:
Genotype 1 and resistance

  • Genotype 1: requires DAAs with pangenotypic or specific (e.g. ledipasvir/sofosbuvir for genotype 1).
  • Resistance associated with mutations in NS5A (e.g. Y93H, L31M) and NS3/4A (Q80K in genotype 1a).
  • Q80K: reduces sensitivity to simeprevir; Screen before use in genotype 1a.
  • Current regimen: glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (8 weeks) or sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (12 weeks) avoid resistance testing.
  • Failure to AAD: genotype and perform rescue resistance testing.

items.title: AAD Hepatitis C
items.contentHtml:
Direct action antivirals Hepatitis C

  • Classes: NS5B (sofosbuvir), NS5A (velpatasvir, pibrentasvir), NS3/4A (glecaprevir, voxilaprevir) inhibitors.
  • Pangenotypic: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (12 weeks), glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (8 weeks without cirrhosis).
  • Contraindications: interaction with amiodarone (bradycardia), rifampicin, St. John's wort.
  • Efficacy: SVR >95% in all genotypes; adjustment in renal failure (avoid sofosbuvir if GFR <30 mL/min).
  • Duration: 8–12 weeks; decompensated cirrhosis: 12 weeks with ribavirin.

items.foco: Hepatitis C screening at risk population
items.title: HCV screening
items.contentHtml:
Hepatitis C screening at risk population

  • Target population: baby boomers (1945–1965), IV drug users, HIV+, hemodialysis, transplant recipients, children of HCV+ mothers.
  • Initial test: anti-HCV (ELISA); if positive, confirm with HCV-RNA (viral load).
  • Anti-HCV negative but high suspicion (immunosuppression): request direct HCV-RNA.
  • Universal screening: recommended at least once in adults (CDC 2020).
  • Periodicity: annual in active drug users or HIV+ with risky practices.

items.title: HIV-HCV co-infection
items.contentHtml:
HIV-HCV co-infection

  • Prevalence: 25–30% of HIV+ have HCV (especially IV drug users).
  • Accelerated progression to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma; higher liver mortality.
  • Treatment: DAA with sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (12 weeks) without adjustment of ART; monitor interaction with TAF and tenofovir.
  • Avoid ribavirin if possible due to anemia; do not use interferon.
  • ART: prefer dolutegravir or raltegravir to avoid hepatotoxicity from nevirapine/efavirenz.
  • Goal: treat HCV regardless of CD4; SVR similar to monoinfected patients (>95%).

items.title: HCV and liver transplant
items.contentHtml:
Hepatitis C and liver transplantation

  • Indication: decompensated cirrhosis or HCV+ hepatocellular carcinoma.
  • Treat HCV before transplant if MELD <20 and time on list; if not, post-transplant.
  • Post-LT regimen: pangenotypic DAAs (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir ± ribavirin) 12 weeks, with monitoring of renal function and interaction with immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine).
  • Post-LT SVR: >90%; reduces risk of rejection and recurrence.
  • Caution: adjust tacrolimus dose due to CYP3A4 inhibition by glecaprevir/pibrentasvir.
